coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of carbon dots from Hakka yellow wine lees: Characterization and applications for Fe3+ and NaFeEDTA sensing
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Hongpeng
Xiang, Yujuan
Wu, Kegang
He, Dong
Chai, Xianghua
Xu, Lin
Cheng, Yongqi
Duan, Xuejuan
Li, Wenhui
coredata.dc:description: In this study, Hakka yellow wine lees carbon dots (HYWL-CDs) with an average diameter of 1.75 nm are synthesized from Hakka yellow wine lees (HYWL) via a one-pot hydrothermal method. Results of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and FTIR reveal the presence of C=C and –OH in the HYWL-CDs. Additionally, the HYWL-CDs present excitation-wavelength dependence, with maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of 360 and 440 nm, respectively. The fluorescence of the HYWL-CDs can be quenched by Fe3+. The Fe3+ concentration is correlated linearly with the quenching rate over a linear range of 0.6–1.1 mg/mL, and the limit of detection is 0.18 μg/mL. And phenols have been shown to be present in HYWL-CDs, and the color reaction between phenols and FeCl3 is most likely the mechanism by which FeCl3 causes fluorescence burst in HYWL-CDs. Moreover, the HYWL-CDs demonstrate significant potential for detecting NaFeEDTA in iron-fortified soy sauces, the linear-fit equation was y = 185.38x + 1024.6 (R2 = 0.9995) The HYWL-CDs are loaded onto paper strips, iron-fortified straw mushroom dark soy sauce showed noticeable color changes, which allows one to differentiate normal soy sauce from iron-fortified soy sauce more conveniently and provides a new perspective for the high-value utilization of yellow wine lees.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Yellow wine lees
Carbon dot
Fluorescent detection
Metal ion quenching
Nanosensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: HYWL-CD strip for selective ion detection
Detection of Fe3+/NaFeEDTA in soy sauce
Interaction study based on different ions and selectivity study

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of synthesis of HYWL-CDs and detection of Fe3+.

Structural characterization of HYWL-CDs. (a) TEM of HYWL-CDs and the corresponding particle size distribution histogram; (b) XRD spectrum of HYWL-CDs; (c) FTIR spectrum of HYWL-CDs; (d) XPS survey spectrum for the HYWL-CDs; (e) High-resolution spectra of C 1s, (f) N 1s, and (g) O 1s.

Optical characterization of HYWL-CDs. (a) UV–vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of HYWL-CDs (Inset: images of HYWL-CDs under daylight and UV light); (b) Fluorescent emission spectra of HYWL-CDs at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 350 nm to 380 nm; (c) Fluorescence quenching efficiencies of HYWL-CDs after addition of potential interferences.
The fluorescence spectra of HYWL-CDs reacting with different concentrations of Fe3+ (a) and Effect of different concentrations of iron ions on the fluorescence intensity of HYWL-CDs (b).

Fluorescence burst mechanism of HYWL-CDs. (a) Effect of iron ions on the Stern-Volmer plots of HYWL-CDs; (b) Effect of iron ions on the double logarithmic plots of HYWL-CDs; (c) Absorption spectra of HYWL-CDs and HYWL-CDs after the addition of iron ions.

Paper strip loaded HYWL-CDs. (a) Digital images of paper strips after incubation with different ions under daylight. (b) Digital images of HYWL-CDs paper strips after incubation with different ions under daylight. (c) Fluorescent images of paper strips under UV lamp irradiation. (d) Fluorescent images of HYWL-CDs paper strips after incubation with different ions under UV lamp irradiation.

Paper strip loaded with HYWL-CDs for soy sauce detection. (a) Effect of different concentrations of NaFeEDTA on the fluorescence intensity of HYWL-CDs. (b) Detection of Fe3+ in soy sauce. (c) Digital images of HYWL-CDs paper strips after incubation with different soy sauce under daylight. (d) Fluorescent images of HYWL-CDs paper strips after incubation with different soy sauce under UV lamp irradiation.
Effect of iron ions on the fluorescence quenching parameters and thermodynamic.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of carbon dots from Hakka yellow wine lees: Characterization and applications for Fe3+ and NaFeEDTA sensing

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, Hakka yellow wine lees carbon dots (HYWL-CDs) with an average diameter of 1.75 nm are synthesized from Hakka yellow wine lees (HYWL) via a one-pot hydrothermal method. Results of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and FTIR reveal the presence of C=C and –OH in the HYWL-CDs. Additionally, the HYWL-CDs present excitation-wavelength dependence, with maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of 360 and 440 nm, respectively. The fluorescence of the HYWL-CDs can be quenched by Fe3+. The Fe3+ concentration is correlated linearly with the quenching rate over a linear range of 0.6–1.1 mg/mL, and the limit of detection is 0.18 μg/mL. And phenols have been shown to be present in HYWL-CDs, and the color reaction between phenols and FeCl3 is most likely the mechanism by which FeCl3 causes fluorescence burst in HYWL-CDs. Moreover, the HYWL-CDs demonstrate significant potential for detecting NaFeEDTA in iron-fortified soy sauces, the linear-fit equation was y = 185.38x + 1024.6 (R2 = 0.9995) The HYWL-CDs are loaded onto paper strips, iron-fortified straw mushroom dark soy sauce showed noticeable color changes, which allows one to differentiate normal soy sauce from iron-fortified soy sauce more conveniently and provides a new perspective for the high-value utilization of yellow wine lees.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Yellow rice wine (Huangjiu) is one of the oldest alcoholic beverages in China and dates back more than 2500 years. (Cai et al., 2019). Guangdong Hakka (Hakka Huangjiu) yellow rice wine, which is an important constituent of Huangjiu wine, is renowned for its delicate flavor, distinctive aroma, and red color; furthermore, it is well recognized worldwide. Hakka Huangjiu is rich in amino acids, vitamins, glucose, organic acids, and other nutrients that can help prevent hypertension thrombosis, coronary heart disease, insomnia, and memory improvement (Zhao et al., 2022). Hakka yellow wine lees (HYWL) are generated during the production of Hakka Huangjiu and can be used as fuel (DeRose et al., 2019) and feed. However, this is not only a low value-added utilization but also easily causes environmental pollution. Currently, studies pertaining to HYWL primarily focus on the extraction and application of nutrients (Yang et al., 2021). To solve the environmental pollution caused by food waste, the deep processing and utilization of food waste should be considered.

Carbon dots (CDs) are photoluminescent materials measuring less than 10 nm (Xu et al., 2004). CDs have garnered significant attention as an emerging fluorescent nanomaterial because of their high chemical stability, good photostability, and low toxicity (Cao et al., 2022). CDs are used as a material in fluorescence detection techniques to achieve lower detection limits, higher detection sensitivities, and shorter detection times, as well as to conserve labor and material resources. Therefore, CDs are highly promising when used in applications such as bio-imaging (Luo et al., 2014), sensing (Yoo et al., 2019), and photocatalysis (Y. N. Liu et al., 2021). Most approaches for the green synthesis of CDs involve either hydrothermal techniques or microwave-assisted synthesis owing to the ease of fabrication and faster synthesis processes afforded (Tyagi et al., 2016). Varisco et al. prepared wine lees CDs using microwave and ultrasonic methods (Varisco et al., 2017). However, the synthesis of Hakka yellow wine lees carbon dots (HYWL-CDs) via a hydrothermal method has not been reported. Therefore, to manage HYWL more conveniently, we used HYWL as a carbon source for the hydrothermal synthesis of HYWL-CDs.

Iron is one of the most abundant elements in the human body and is vital to metabolism. Fe3+ deficiency can adversely affect human health and may result in certain diseases such as venous thrombosis (Ezeh et al., 2021), Alzheimer's disease (Levine, 2023), gastrointestinal diseases (Rodríguez-Moranta et al., 2014), and heart failure (Manceau et al., 2022). Eating iron-fortified foods is an economical, effective, and sustainable option for preventing these diseases and ensuring adequate iron intake. Different forms of iron have been used to fortify food products (Man et al., 2022). NaFeEDTA is a more widely used iron fortifier because it causes fewer sensory problems than other water-soluble iron compounds (Martínez-Navarrete et al., 2002). Heavy-metal pollution caused by excessive Fe content is an extremely concerning problem that must be solved (Sarhat & Al-Obaidi, 2023). In this regard, the detection of Fe is essential. Currently, the main techniques for detecting iron ions include atomic absorption spectrometry (Acar et al., 2023), ion chromatography (D. Liu et al., 2023), spectrophotometry (Al-Batty et al., 2022), and liquid chromatography (Proch & Niedzielski, 2021). However, these methods present disadvantages such as complex instrument use, cumbersome sample-preparation procedures, and high costs, which limit their application in the real world. Thus, a rapid, sensitive, and low-cost method for detecting Fe ions must be developed.

In this study, HYWL-CDs are synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal method using yellow wine lees as the carbon and nitrogen source. HYWL-CDs are uniformly dispersed in water, present an average diameter of 1.7 ± 0.4 nm, and contains numerous functional groups on their surface. They emit blue fluorescence and are selective for Fe3+. The fluorescence is quenched dynamically by adding Fe3+. Subsequently, the HYWL-CDs are loaded onto paper strips to enable the portable detection of Fe3+. In particular, the HYWL-CDs are used to detect Fe3+ in iron-fortified soy sauce.

HYWL were obtained from Jinhuangtian Liquor Co. Ltd. (Heyuan, China). Na2CO3, CuCl2, NaCl, FeCl2, KCl, BaCl2, AlCl3, NH4Cl, and FeCl3 were obtained from Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). NaFeEDTA and Folin–Ciocalteu phenol reagents were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Gallic acid was obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Haitian zero-added gold-label light soy sauce and Haitian iron-fortified straw mushroom dark soy sauce (IF-DSS) were purchased from Foshan Haitian Flavoring and Food Co., Ltd. (Foshan, China). Chubang Little Naughty Soy Sauce was obtained from Guangdong Meiweixian Flavoring Foods Co., Ltd. (Zhongshan, China). Deionized water is prepared by the electrode deionizer produced by Guangzhou Feilong Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Guangzhou, China).

The morphology and size of the HYWL-CDs synthesized were characterized via transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Talos F200S transmission electron microscope, Thermo, US) at an acceleration voltage of 100 kV. The infrared spectrum of the HYWL-CDs was obtained using an iS50R FTIR spectrometer (Thermo, US), where scanning was performed from 500 to 4000 cm−1 using KBr as a reference. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the HYWL-CDs were recorded on a Rigaku diffractometer (Ultima III, Japan) with monochromatized CuKα graphite (λ = 0.15405 nm) from 0° to 80°. The CDs were subjected to X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Escalab 250Xi, Thermo, US), where a vacuum level exceeding 5.0 × 10−10 mbar was implemented in the analytical chamber. Additionally, they were analyzed based on a 650 μm spot at the X-ray source (Al target) under a voltage and current of 15 kV and 15 mA, respectively. Fluorescence analysis was performed using a Varioskan LUX microplate reader (Thermo Fisher Scientific).

HYWL-CDs were synthesized with reference to the method of Amin et al. with appropriate modifications (Amin et al., 2018). HYWL-CDs with a blue glow were synthesized via a hydrothermal method using a single boiler. After the HYWL were centrifuged to remove the supernatant, 2 g of the solid was dissolved in 60 mL of water and then sonicated for 20 min. Subsequently, the solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave (100 mL) and incubated at 200 °C for 6 h. The solution was removed from the oven and cooled in a fume hood for 2 h, then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min, and the supernatant was filtered with a tabletop suction device using a filter membrane with a pore size of 0.22 μm. The filtrate was dialyzed using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 500 Da for 48 h, with several changes of water in the middle. At the end of dialysis, the obtained lees carbon quantum dots were collected. The obtained lees carbon quantum dot solution was stored at 4 °C for two months.

Methodology based on Edison et al. with appropriate modifications (Edison et al., 2016). Different concentrations of Fe3+ solution were mixed with the HYWL-CD solution in equal amounts, and their emission spectra were recorded between 380 and 600 nm with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. All experiments were performed at least thrice.

ce:section.ce:section-title: HYWL-CD strip for selective ion detection
ce:section.ce:para: The methodology is based on the previous report with some modifications (Rajendran et al., 2021). Whatman cellulose filter paper was cut into small pieces. The paper strips were soaked in the HYWL-CD solution for 5 min and then dried. Subsequently, they were immersed in different ionic solutions and the fluorescence burst was monitored using an ultraviolet (UV) analyzer at 365 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+/NaFeEDTA in soy sauce
ce:section.ce:para: Fe3+/NaFeEDTA in soy sauce was detected via an approach similar to that described in Section 2.4. The HYWL-CD solution was mixed with the three types of soy sauce in equal amounts, and its fluorescence intensity was recorded and analyzed.

Statistical analysis was performed using the IBM SPSS statistical program (SPSS 25.0; SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA). Duncan's test was used for statistical analysis. A p-value <0.05 was considered significant.

Wine lees, as a byproduct of food processing, are abundant in carbon and nitrogen, thus facilitating the synthesis of CDs (Fig. 1 ). Hydrothermal carbonization is a typical wine lees carbonization process, where chars are chemically derived from carbohydrates and protein reactants (Heilmann et al., 2011). HYWL-CDs were formed by heating the solution as it transformed into a brownish–yellow hue (data not shown). Aqueous solutions of the HYWL-CDs emitted a bright blue light when irradiated with a UV analyzer. Carbon precursors form carbon nuclei at high temperature and HYWL-CDs as time progresses (Ali et al., 2016).

TEM was performed to characterize the particle-size distribution and morphology of the HYWL-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the HYWL-CDs were uniformly spherical in shape, with well dispersed particles measuring 1.75 nm on average, which is consistent with the definition of quantum dots. The structure was analyzed via XRD (Fig. 2b), and the XRD pattern showed an amorphous carbon dispersion peak at approximately 2θ = 20°, thus indicating that the synthesized HYWL-CDs were amorphous. The elemental composition of the HYWL-CDs was investigated via XPS (Fig. 2d). The full XPS spectrum show C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s peaks at 284.81, 399.81 and 531.48 eV, respectively. The deconvoluted C 1s spectrum shows three peaks with binding energies of 284.80, 286.17, and 288.03 eV (Fig. 2e), which correspond to C–C, C–O/C–N, and C=O, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2f–C-N appeared owing to a peak in the N 1s spectrum at 399.80 eV. Meanwhile, C=O and C-O corresponded to deconvolved O 1s peaks at 531.34 and 532.59 eV, respectively (Fig. 2g). In addition, the FTIR results (Fig. 2c) show that C=O and –NO2 were located at 1670 and 1591 cm−1, respectively, thus indicating that the hydrothermal method successfully carbonized the HYWL and that numerous groups were present on the surface of the synthesized substance, thus corroborating the XPS results.The C=O can coordinate with metal ions, leading to fluorescence burst and realizing selective recognition of iron ions. In addition, the –NO2 on the benzene ring can also react with iron under acidic conditions, and the two have been hypothesized to be the mechanism by which Fe3+ lead to the fluorescence burst of HYWL-CDs. These results indicate that HYWL-CDs were successfully synthesized.

In terms of optical characterization, the absorption bands of the UV–Vis spectrum (Fig. 3 a) corresponded to the n–π* transition of C=O, which was centered at 330 nm. In addition, the excitation spectrum exhibited a peak at 361 nm, which corresponded to the emission peak at 432 nm. The inset shows the photographs of the HYWL-CDs solution under daylight and UV lamps (365 nm). It fluoresced blue under excitation at 365 nm and brownish yellow in daylight. Fig. 3b shows that when the excitation wavelength was 350–360 nm, the fluorescence intensity of the HYWL-CDs increased with the excitation wavelength, and a slight redshift occurred. When the excitation wavelength was 360–380 nm, the fluorescence intensity of the HYWL-CDs decreased as the excitation wavelength increased, and a significant redshift occurred. This indicates that the HYWL-CDs were affected by the excitation wavelength. In addition, an excitation wavelength of 360 nm was used for the subsequent experiments as the maximum fluorescence intensity was indicated at this wavelength.

After the successful synthesis of the HYWL-CDs, their photoluminescent stability was evaluated as follows: First, a UV analyzer was used to continuously irradiate the HYWL-CDs solution to evaluate its photostability. As shown in Fig. S2a, after 80 min of excitation, the fluorescence intensity of the HYWL-CDs reduced by only 20%, thus indicating their favorable anti-photobleaching capability. Second, the fluorescence properties were affected by the pH level. Fig. S2b shows that the fluorescence intensity remained stable within pH 3–7. However, at pH 9, the surface groups and FL intensity changed, which might be caused by a disruption to the ionization equilibrium of the solution (Dmitrieva et al., 2020). As shown in Fig. S2c, the FL intensity remained almost constant over the NaCl concentration range of 0–1 mol/L, thus indicating that the HYWL-CDs can withstand high salt concentrations. In addition, when the temperature increased to 55 °C, the FL intensity remained greater than 70%, thus signifying their high-temperature resistance. These excellent features prove that the HYWL-CDs possess excellent photoluminescence properties and may be a promising material for developing effective fluorescence detection methods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction study based on different ions and selectivity study
ce:section.ce:para: The HYWL-CDs solution was mixed with various metal and nonmetal components to test their selective recognition. Aqueous suspensions of HYWL-CDs were mixed with a series of metal and nonmetal ions to investigate their selective sensing applications. Specific concentrations (1 mg/mL) of Cu2+, Na+, Fe2+, K+, Ba2+, Al3+, NH4 +, and Fe3+ ions were mixed separately with the HYWL-CDs solution, and the fluorescence was measured immediately. As shown in Fig. 3c, the addition of Fe3+ caused significant fluorescence quenching, whereas the addition of other species showed less significant effects. The addition of K+, NH4 +, Ba2+, and Al3+ caused fluorescence enhancement, which might be due to the surface plasmon effect of carbon quantum dots (Guo et al., 2015).

After confirming the specific response of the HYWL-CDs to Fe3+, the sensitivity of the fluorescent probe was investigated. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the FL intensity of the HYWL-CDs at 360 nm decreased as the Fe3+ concentration increased. The Fe3+ concentration showed a highly linear relationship with the quenching rate over a linear range of 0.6–1.1 mg/mL, i.e., the linear-fit equation was y = 542.86x + 407.83 (R2 = 0.993). Meanwhile, the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.18 μg/mL (2.9 × 10−7 μM). A comparison between the analytical capabilities of the HYWL-CDs with other CDs is summarized in Table S1. Whereas the synthesis of most CDs require more than one chemical, the synthesis of HYWL-CDs is simple and safe because only food waste is used as the precursor. Additionally, the HYWL-CDs present a similar LOD and a relatively wider linear range compared with other CDs, which demonstrates their potential as an ideal material for the sensitive and efficient analysis of Fe3+. Meanwhile, the FTIR results revealed the presence of C=C and –OH; this implies that any compound with a C=C–OH structure can form a colored complex with FeCl3 and that the HYWL-CDs might have reacted with FeCl3. The total phenolic content of the HYWL-CDs was determined using a modified version of the method described by Rimpapa et al. (Rimpapa et al., 2007). Fig. S1 shows the standard curve for the determination of polyphenol content (y = 0.257x + 0.04; R2 = 0.998). The total phenolic content of the HYWL-CDs was 0.205 mg/mL, i.e., the total phenol content in 1 mL of the HYWL-CDs was 0.41 mg, thus suggesting that the speculation above may be valid. Additionally, the color change observed in the strip test supports this speculation.

We investigated the possible mechanism by which the HYWL-CDs reacted with Fe3+. The quenching mechanism of the HYWL-CDs by Fe3+ was analyzed using the Stern–Volmer equation, as shown in Equation (1).
(1)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
S V
[ Q ]
= 1 +
K q
τ 0
[ Q ]
,
where F0 represents the fluorescence intensity without a quencher, F the fluorescence intensity with a quencher, Ksv the dynamic quenching constant, Kq the quenching rate constant, τ0 the fluorescence lifetime (1 × 10−8s), and [Q] the Fe3+ concentration (mg/mL).

As shown in Fig. 5 a, at 299, 305, and 311 K, the slope of the Stern–Volmer curve increased with temperature; in other words, the dynamic quenching constant Ksv increased, thus indicating that the two were determined by the interaction of dynamic quenching mechanisms (Wang et al., 2016). Fig. 5c shows that the absorbance of the ML-CDs changed slightly after the addition of Fe3+ as a quencher. However, the position of the absorption peak remained unchanged, thus indicating that the addition of Fe3+ did not affect the structure of the ML-CDs, which further verified the dynamic quenching mechanism.

Based on the findings above, the Stern–Volmer curves can be deformed into double logarithmic curves using Scatchard's equation, as shown in Equation (2) (Fig. 5b).
(2)
log
F 0
F
= log
K a
+ n
log
[ Q ]
The number of binding sites (n) and the apparent binding constant (Ka) were calculated using Equation (2) (Table 1 ).

To determine the type of interaction force, the thermodynamic parameters were calculated using the van Hoff equation.
(3)
l n K a = −
Δ
H °
R T
+
Δ
S °
R
(4)
Δ
G °
= Δ
H °
− T Δ
S °
,
where K
a is the apparent binding constant (L/mol), H° the enthalpy change (kJ/g), S° the entropy change (J/g/K), G° the Gibbs free energy (kJ/g), T the interaction temperature (K), and R the gas constant (8.314 J/K/mol).

As shown in Table 1, n was greater than 1, thus indicating that more than one binding site existed between the HYWL-CDs and Fe3+. The apparent binding constant, Ka, was substituted into Equation (4) to obtain the relevant thermodynamic parameters to determine the type of interaction between the HYWL-CDs and Fe3+. Electrostatic, van der Waals, hydrogen-bonding, and hydrophobic-interaction forces are the four most common intermolecular forces, and the main binding forces between the HYWL-CDs and Fe3+ can be assessed based on the positive and negative effects of ΔH and ΔS (Wu et al., 2018). Here, ΔH > 0 and ΔS < 0 indicate that the main force between the two is electrostatic, whereas ΔG > 0 indicates that the combination of the two is a nonspontaneous reaction process (Jia et al., 2017). In addition, a positive ΔH° value indicates that the binding process is an endothermic reaction, which can explain the positive correlation between temperature and Ksv when the HYWL-CDs interact with Fe3+.

Paper strip sensors were developed to enable the use of HYWL-CDs in on-site fields and actual applications. As shown in Fig. 6 c, the paper strips that were not soaked in the HYWL-CDs solution did not fluoresce under a UV lamp (365 nm), whereas the paper strips soaked in the HYWL-CDs solution showed blue fluorescence under UV light (365 nm). The paper strip impregnated with Fe3+ showed notable color changes, whereas the other metal and nonmetal ions exhibited strong blue fluorescence only (Fig. 6d). Fig. 6a shows a set of daylight comparisons. In addition to loading CDs onto paper strips, they can be loaded onto silica aerogels, which enables the detection of specific ions via color changes (Rajendran et al., 2021). By contrast, the load on the paper strip is not only easy to prepare and transport but also offers advantages in practical applications. We achieved the rapid detection of iron ions in soy sauce using this method, which is considerably important for the reuse of wine lees and for the testing of food ingredients. In summary, this study provides a new idea for the practical application of CDs and demonstrates their portable and extensive application.

To evaluate the applicability of the proposed method, three types of soy sauce were added with Fe3+ to perform detection. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the FL intensity of the HYWL-CDs at 360 nm decreased as the NaFeEDTA concentration increased. The NaFeEDTA concentration showed a highly linear relationship with the quenching rate over a linear range of 0.2–1.0 mg/mL, i.e., the linear-fit equation was y = 185.38x + 1024.6 (R2 = 0.9995), thus demonstrating the significant potential of the HYWL-CDs for NaFeEDTA detection. As shown in Fig. 7b, the HYWL-CDs solution showed different degrees of fluorescence in response to the three types of soy sauce, among which the fluorescence value of the IF-DSS decreased significantly (p > 0.05). This indicates that the HYWL-CDs can recognize both normal soy sauce and iron-fortified soy sauce, thus further demonstrating their significant potential for detecting NaFeEDTA. However, because of the composition of soy sauce is complex, achieving accurate detections is challenging; thus, further investigations are necessitated to achieve breakthroughs. In addition, different types of soy sauce were dipped into the HYWL-CDs paper strips. All of them showed noticeable color changes, particularly the IF-DSS, which transformed from deep purple in daylight (Fig. 7c) and caused fluorescence quenching under UV light (365 nm) (Fig. 7d). Hence, it can be used to conveniently distinguish between regular soy sauce and iron-fortified soy sauce, thus providing new perspectives for the development of wine lees.

In this study, we synthesized high-quality HYWL-CDs via a one-step hydrothermal method using food waste HYWL as the precursor. The HYWL-CDs nanoparticles were evenly sized spheres with numerous groups on the surface and possessed excellent photoluminescence properties. XPS and FTIR results revealed the presence of C=C and –OH on the surface. The HYWL-CDs exhibited excitation wavelength dependence, with maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of 360 and 440 nm, respectively. The fluorescence of the HYWL-CDs was successfully quenched by Fe3+, and the quenching rate exhibited a highly linear relationship with the concentration of Fe3+ in the linear range of 0.6–1.1 mg/mL, with a LOD of 0.18 μg/mL. Phenols were present in the HYWL-CDs, and the color reaction between phenols and FeCl3 was the most likely mechanism by which FeCl3 resulted in fluorescence burst in the HYWL-CDs. Moreover, the HYWL-CDs indicated considerable potential for detecting NaFeEDTA in iron-fortified soy sauce, with different concentrations of NaFeEDTA reacting with them showing an excellent linear relationship. However, due to the complexity of soy sauce composition, it is challenging to achieve accurate detection, and thus further research is needed to achieve a breakthrough. In conclusion, the HYWL-CDs demonstrated significant potential for the practical detection of Fe3+ and NaFeEDTA, thus providing a new perspective on expanding the use of food waste for detection.

Hongpeng Yu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Yujuan Xiang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Kegang Wu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Dong He: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Xianghua Chai: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Lin Xu: Methodology. Yongqi Cheng: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Xuejuan Duan: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Wenhui Li: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Science and technology plan project of Heyuan City (220705101471437), Science & Technology Planning Project of Guangzhou City (202103000078, 20226010181).

ce:section.ce:para:
